children.value: Imagine you are an environmental scientist studying a lake. At a single point in the lake, you have a probe that measures 50 different things simultaneously: water temperature, pH, dissolved oxygen, turbidity, conductivity, and concentrations of 45 different chemical pollutants. You collect this 50-dimensional data point every minute for a year.
Now you have millions of data points, each with 50 features. Your goal is simple: ‘How did the lake’s overall health change over the year?’

Answering this is impossible by plotting 50 separate graphs. Many of these features are redundant (e.g., several pollutants might come from the same source and always appear together). How can we boil these 50 correlated features down to just two or three ‘meta-features’ that capture the most important patterns of change? For instance, perhaps we could discover a ‘Pollution Event Axis’ and a ‘Seasonal Temperature Axis’.

This is the core problem that Principal Component Analysis solves. It is a powerful method for finding the most meaningful ‘meta-features’ (the Principal Components) in complex, high-dimensional data, allowing us to visualize patterns and understand the hidden structure of our measurements.

The common goal of dimensionality reduction is to transform high-dimensional data into a lower-dimensional space while preserving as much of the meaningful structure and properties of the original data as possible. This is done to simplify datasets, reduce computational and storage costs, and make data easier to visualize and analyze. The following list show some of the more relevant methods used:
A linear technique that transforms the data into a new coordinate system of orthogonal axes called principal components.
These components are ordered by the amount of variance they capture from the original data
linear technique that finds a linear combination of features that best separates two or more classes of objects. It aims to
maximize the between-class variance while minimizing the within-class variance
A non-linear method primarily used for
. It models data points by their similarities in both high and low dimensions,
preserving the local structure of the data
A modern non-linear technique that constructs a
graph representation of the data in high dimensions and then optimizes a similar graph in a lower dimension
. It is known for its speed and ability to preserve both local and global data structure. Reference:
An extension of PCA that uses kernel functions to
. It implicitly maps the data to a higher-dimensional space where linear separation is possible, then applies PCA. Reference:
A type of neural network used for unsupervised learning. It consists of an
“encoder” that compresses the input into a low-dimensional code and a “decoder” that reconstructs the input from this code
. The compressed code is the lower-dimensional representation. Reference:
This is a category of methods that
select a subset of the original features

rather than creating new ones. Techniques include filter methods (e.g., chi-squared test), wrapper methods (e.g., recursive feature elimination), and embedded methods (e.g., LASSO regularization). Reference:
on data manifold using neighborhood graph + MDS.
Simple, fast, and removes correlated features. Effective for linear data.
Can be sensitive to data scaling. Performs poorly with non-linear data.
Improves classification performance by focusing on class separability.
Supervised (requires labels). Assumes data is normally distributed.
Excellent at visualizing local clusters in high-dimensional data.
Computationally expensive. Can lose global structure information.
Very fast and scalable. Preserves both local and global data structure.
Can be sensitive to hyperparameters. Interpretation can be nuanced.
Can capture complex, non-linear relationships in the data.
More computationally intensive than PCA. Requires careful kernel selection.
Can learn highly complex, non-linear representations. Flexible for various data types.
Can be prone to overfitting. Requires a large amount of training data.
Improves model interpretability by using original features. Reduces overfitting.
May discard features that are useful only in combination with others.
aptures global nonlinear structure; preserves manifold geometry well if assumptions hold.
(Classic references like “Isomap: Learning Nonlinear Structure from High‑Dimensional Data”, etc.) — not explicitly in the small set I pulled but widely cited.
Many scientific datasets have many correlated measurements: spectra (many wavelengths), climate grids (many locations), images (many pixels), recordings from many sensors or neurons.
PCA finds orthogonal directions (principal components) that explain the most variance, which helps visualization, noise reduction, compression, and discovering latent degrees of freedom.

Principal Component Analysis (PCA) is a statistical technique used to reduce the dimensionality of data while preserving as much variance as possible. Think of it as finding the best camera angle to capture the most information about a 3D object in a 2D photograph.
Dimensionality Reduction: Transform high-dimensional data to lower dimensions
Variance Preservation: Keep the most important patterns in the data
Data Visualization: Make complex datasets interpretable
Noise Reduction: Filter out less important variations
Identify key wavelengths in complex spectra
Find patterns in gene expression data
Identify gene clusters, reduce computational complexity
Understand weather patterns from multiple variables
Optimize reaction conditions, understand structure-property relationships
Extract signals from noise, identify fundamental modes

Higa, G. S., de Oliveira, L. G., Luns, K. F., de Jesus, R. E., Ferreira, R. C., da Silva, J. A., Cunha, P. H., & Pires, D. S. (2024). Application of Principal Component Analysis as a Prediction Model for Feline Sporotrichosis. Animals, 14(12), 1696.
Sfriso, P., & Crave, A. (2022). Principal Component Analysis and Related Methods for Investigating the Dynamics of Biological Macromolecules. J - Multidisciplinary Scientific Journal, 5(2), 298–317.
Huang, J., Lu, D., Lin, W., & Yang, Q. (2024). Enhancing slope stability prediction through integrated PCA-SSA-SVM modeling: a case study of LongLian expressway. Frontiers in Earth Science, 12. DOI:
Kaur, L., & Godara, P. (2025). Application of Principal Component Analysis (PCA) in groundwater quality evaluation: A case study of arid region. Energy & Environment Management, 1(21).

Kahangwa, C. (2022). Application of Principal Component Analysis, Cluster Analysis, Pollution Index and Geoaccumulation Index in Pollution Assessment with Heavy Metals from Gold Mining Operations, Tanzania. Journal of Geoscience and Environment Protection, 10(4), 303-317.

Moreira, M., Hillenkamp, M., Divitini, G., Tizei, L. H. G., Ducati, C., Cotta, M. A., Rodrigues, V., & Ugarte, D. (2025). Improving Quantitative EDS Chemical Analysis of Alloy Nanoparticles by PCA Denoising: Part I, Reducing Reconstruction Bias. Microscopy and Microanalysis. DOI:
Yao, Z., Yang, J., & Lin, H.-Q. (2025). Principal component analysis for percolation with and without data preprocessing. Physical Review E, 111(4).DOI:

children.value: Use the largests covariance matrix eigen values to select a few PC only (Why?)

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from ipywidgets import interact

# Generate correlated data
np.random.seed(0)
mean = [0, 0]
cov = [[3, 2], [2, 2]]  # correlated
X = np.random.multivariate_normal(mean, cov, size=300)

# Center data (important!)
X = X - X.mean(axis=0)

def plot_projection(theta_deg=0):
    theta = np.deg2rad(theta_deg)
    u = np.array([np.cos(theta), np.sin(theta)])  # direction

    # Project data
    z = X @ u
    var = np.var(z)

    plt.figure(figsize=(6,6))
    plt.scatter(X[:,0], X[:,1], alpha=0.3)

    # Plot axis
    line = np.linspace(-5, 5, 100)
    axis = np.outer(line, u)
    plt.plot(axis[:,0], axis[:,1], 'r', label='Projection axis')

    plt.title(f"Angle = {theta_deg:.1f}°, Variance = {var:.2f}")
    plt.xlim(-6,6)
    plt.ylim(-6,6)
    plt.legend()
    plt.grid()
    plt.gca().set_aspect('equal', adjustable='box')
    plt.show()

interact(plot_projection, theta_deg=(0,180,1));

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Data generation
np.random.seed(0)
n = 400
x = np.random.normal(size=n)
y = 2.0 * x + 0.8 * np.random.normal(size=n)
X = np.vstack([x,y]).T
Xc = X - X.mean(axis=0)

plt.figure(figsize=(6,6))
plt.scatter(Xc[:,0], Xc[:,1], s=15)
plt.xlabel('x (centered)')
plt.ylabel('y (centered)')
plt.title('2D data')
plt.axis('equal')
plt.grid(True)
plt.show()
This data is clearly positively correlated. Before anything, we can perform several previous anaylsis to get a better idea about the data.
Let’s plot each variable against the other in the so-called pair plot (

import pandas as pd

# Create a DataFrame with the centered data
df = pd.DataFrame(Xc, columns=['x', 'y'])

# Pairplot to visualize pairwise relationships and distributions
sns.pairplot(df)
plt.show()
The correlation among variables, defined as (

children.value:
# Compute the covariance matrix, and its eigen values and vectors, ordered
# rowvar = False means vars are in columns in this case
C = np.cov(Xc, rowvar=False)

# Plot the covariance matrix as a heatmap
plt.figure(figsize=(6, 6))
sns.heatmap(C, annot=True, cmap="coolwarm", cbar=True, square=True, fmt=".2f", 
            xticklabels=["x", "y"], yticklabels=["x", "y"])
plt.title("Covariance Matrix Heatmap")
plt.show()
As you can see, it is related to the correlation matrix as

children.html: Corr(X,Y)=Cov(X,Y)Var(X)Var(Y).Corr(X, Y) = \frac{Cov(X, Y)}{\sqrt{Var(X)Var(Y)}}.Corr(X,Y)=Var(X)Var(Y)​Cov(X,Y)​.

children.value: The information stored in the C-eigenvalues and eigenvectors

children.label: The information stored in the C-eigenvalues and eigenvectors

children.value: Computing the covariance-matrix eigenvalues and eigenvectors provide key information about the data spread, and it is the heart of the PCA algorithm. For instance, the following shows how to compute and plot the eigen vals in our problem

from bokeh.plotting import figure, show
from bokeh.io import output_notebook
import numpy as np

# Compute the covariance matrix, and its eigen values and vectors
eigvals, eigvecs = np.linalg.eigh(C)
order = eigvals.argsort()[::-1]
eigvals = eigvals[order]
eigvecs = eigvecs[:, order]

# print the eigevals normalized by the total sum
print(f"Eigenvals proportion: {eigvals/np.sum(eigvals)}")

# Bokeh plot setup
output_notebook()  # Ensure the plot is rendered inline

# Create figure
p = figure(width=600, height=600, title="2D Correlated Data and PC1 and PC2", match_aspect=True)
p.scatter(Xc[:, 0], Xc[:, 1], size=8, color="blue", alpha=0.5)


# Plot the principal component lines
scale = 1
pc1 = eigvecs[:, 0]
print(pc1)
p.line([-scale * eigvals[0] * pc1[0], +scale * eigvals[0] * pc1[0]], 
       [-scale * eigvals[0] * pc1[1], +scale * eigvals[0] * pc1[1]], 
       line_width=3, color="red", alpha=0.9)

pc2 = eigvecs[:, 1]
print(pc2)
scale = 6
p.line([-scale * eigvals[1] * pc2[0], +scale * eigvals[1] * pc2[0]], 
       [-scale * eigvals[1] * pc2[1], +scale * eigvals[1] * pc2[1]], 
       line_width=3, color="orange", alpha=0.9)

# Labels and formatting
p.xaxis.axis_label = "x (centered)"
p.yaxis.axis_label = "y (centered)"
p.grid.grid_line_color = "white"
p.title.text_font_size = '30px'
p.grid.grid_line_color = "gray"  # Grid line color
p.grid.grid_line_alpha = 0.3  # Transparency of grid lines
p.grid.grid_line_width = 1  # Width of grid lines

# Show plot
show(p)

children.value: is very large, it suggest that the problem is ill-represented and there might be multi-linearities (high correlation among features).
The C-determinant speaks about the volume in feature space, and small value might indicate that the data is redundant.
PCA as a constrained optimization problem

children.label: PCA as a constrained optimization problem

children.value: PCA is often viewed as looking for the directions with the most variance. But it can also be formulated as  an optimization problem, and this explains why is the covariance matrix actually there. First, let’s assume all data is centered, that is it has mean 0. Now, let’s also assume that there is a new given unit vector

children.html: uuu

children.value: that could be more useful that some of the original axis we were using. The projection of the features vector

children.html: xxx
uuu

children.value: x' = (u\cdot x) u = (u^T x) u = x_u u.
children.html: x′=(u⋅x)u=(uTx)u=xuu.x' = (u\cdot x) u = (u^T x) u = x_u u.x′=(u⋅x)u=(uTx)u=xu​u.

children.value: Remember that the dot product represents the projection of a vector into another. Check

The dot product represents the amount of information we are retaining, it is maximum when both vectors are in the same direction, and null when they are orthogonal. Since it could be negative, we can define the aount of preserved information as

children.html: (u⋅x)2,(u\cdot x)^2,(u⋅x)2,

children.value: or, better, the mean preserved info over all samples as

\frac{1}{n-1}\sum_i^n (u\cdot x_i)^2 = \frac{1}{n-1} \sum_i (x_{i,u})^2 = \frac{1}{n-1} \sum_i x_{i,u}^T x_{i,u} = \frac{1}{n-1} \sum_i (x_i^T u)(u^T x_i) = \frac{1}{n-1} \sum_i (x_i^T x_i) u u^T = \frac{1}{n-1} \sum_i (x_i^T x_i) u^2, = C u^2,
children.html: 1n−1∑in(u⋅xi)2=1n−1∑i(xi,u)2=1n−1∑ixi,uTxi,u=1n−1∑i(xiTu)(uTxi)=1n−1∑i(xiTxi)uuT=1n−1∑i(xiTxi)u2,=Cu2,\frac{1}{n-1}\sum_i^n (u\cdot x_i)^2 = \frac{1}{n-1} \sum_i (x_{i,u})^2 = \frac{1}{n-1} \sum_i x_{i,u}^T x_{i,u} = \frac{1}{n-1} \sum_i (x_i^T u)(u^T x_i) = \frac{1}{n-1} \sum_i (x_i^T x_i) u u^T = \frac{1}{n-1} \sum_i (x_i^T x_i) u^2, = C u^2,n−11​i∑n​(u⋅xi​)2=n−11​i∑​(xi,u​)2=n−11​i∑​xi,uT​xi,u​=n−11​i∑​(xiT​u)(uTxi​)=n−11​i∑​(xiT​xi​)uuT=n−11​i∑​(xiT​xi​)u2,=Cu2,

children.value: where we have replaced the definition of the covariance matrix

children.html: C=1n−1∑i(xiTxi).C = \frac{1}{n-1} \sum_i (x_i^T x_i).C=n−11​i∑​(xiT​xi​).

children.value: Therefore, to find the direction that preserve the most information about our original data, we must solve the optimization problem

children.html: max⁡Cu2\max Cu^2maxCu2
u2=1.u^2 = 1.u2=1.

children.value: This can be donde using the method of Lagrange multipliers, and some matrix calculus allows us to write it here simply:

children.html: max⁡Cu2,s.t,   u2=1,\max Cu^2, \text{s.t, \ \ } u^2 = 1,maxCu2,s.t,   u2=1,

children.value: deriving with respect to u (yes, it is a vector, see matrix calculus), and introducing a Lagrange multiplier

children.html: λ\lambdaλ
∂∂u(Cu2−λ(u2−1))=0,\frac{\partial}{\partial u} (Cu^2 - \lambda (u^2 - 1)) = 0,∂u∂​(Cu2−λ(u2−1))=0,
2Cu−2λu=0,2Cu - 2\lambda u = 0,2Cu−2λu=0,
Cu=λu,Cu = \lambda u,Cu=λu,

children.value: which is exactly the eigen-value problem for the covariance matrix. Therefore, the directions that preserve the most information about our original data are exactly the eigen-vectors of the covariance matrix.

Standard PCA is sensitive to outliers.
When PCA May Not Work Well
When rare events are important (PCA focuses on major patterns)
: Use median instead of mean, robust covariance estimation
: Assume many loadings are exactly zero

How much variance does each PC explain?
An ecologist runs a PCA on a dataset with 10 features related to forest health. The explained variance for each component is:
Create a scree plot (bar chart of explained variance).
Using the “elbow method,” how many principal components would you choose to retain for further analysis?
What is the cumulative variance explained by your chosen number of components? Is this likely sufficient?
Applied Example: PCA applied to real data

children.label: Applied Example: PCA applied to real data

children.value: Here we will use tha sky data survey to survey objects like start, galaxy and quasar. The goal is to reduce the amount of data dimensions needed to be able to distinguish among them, using PCA.
To get the data, go to
and write or use the default query. To get the full data, use the following sql command

SELECT TOP 10000
    p.ra, p.dec, p.u, p.g, p.r, p.i, p.z,
    p.run, p.rerun, p.camcol, p.field,
    s.specobjid, s.class, s.z as redshift
FROM PhotoObj AS p
JOIN SpecObj AS s ON s.bestobjid = p.objid
WHERE
    p.u BETWEEN 0 AND 19.6
    AND p.g BETWEEN 0 AND 20
This table describes each variable meaning
The celestial equivalent of longitude. It measures the angular distance eastward along the celestial equator.
The celestial equivalent of latitude. It measures the angular distance north (+) or south (-) of the celestial equator.
Ultraviolet filter (central wavelength ~354 nm). Measures light in the near-UV.
Green filter (central wavelength ~475 nm). Measures light in the blue-green spectrum.
Red filter (central wavelength ~622 nm). Often used as the primary band for detecting objects.
Far Red filter (central wavelength ~763 nm). Measures light in the near-infrared spectrum.
Near Infrared filter (central wavelength ~905 nm). Measures the reddest part of the optical spectrum.
Identifies the specific scan (night of observation) during which the image was taken.
Indicates how many times the data from that specific run has been processed/re-analyzed.
The SDSS camera has 6 columns of CCDs. This identifies which column (1-6) detected the object.
Each scan is divided into a series of overlapping rectangular frames called fields.
A unique 64-bit identifier for the spectroscopic record (linked to the physical fiber used).
The classification of the object based on its spectrum (e.g., STAR, GALAXY, or QSO/Quasar).
A measure of how much the light from an object is shifted toward longer wavelengths, used to calculate distance and velocity.
These are magnitudes. In astronomy, lower numbers represent brighter objects.
These are technical tracking variables used to locate the raw image data in the SDSS archive.
These variables come from the spectroscopic data (hence the
prefix), which provides much more detailed information than the photometric images (
This is a bokeh plotting version with some additional info

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
from bokeh.plotting import figure, show, output_notebook
from bokeh.palettes import Category10
from bokeh.models import ColumnDataSource, HoverTool
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

output_notebook()

# --- CONFIG ---
# file_path = 'skyserverdata2.csv'  # Or whatever you named the downloaded file
# # url = "https://drive.google.com/file/d/1ZLnCVim0P1Ktewyhd1VcRNWTDCte-W0O/view?usp=drive_link"
# #file_path = "https://drive.google.com/file/d/1ZLnCVim0P1Ktewyhd1VcRNWTDCte-W0O/view"
# file_path = "https://drive.usercontent.google.com/download?id=1ZLnCVim0P1Ktewyhd1VcRNWTDCte-W0O&export=download"
# #file_path = "https://drive.google.com/uc?id=1ZLnCVim0P1Ktewyhd1VcRNWTDCte-W0O&export=download"
DATA_URL = "https://drive.usercontent.google.com/download?id=1ZLnCVim0P1Ktewyhd1VcRNWTDCte-W0O&export=download"
FEATURES = ['u', 'g', 'r', 'i', 'z']
TARGET = 'class'


def load_data(file_path: str) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Load and validate the SDSS dataset."""
    df = pd.read_csv(file_path, skiprows=1)
    missing = [c for c in FEATURES + [TARGET] if c not in df.columns]
    if missing:
        raise ValueError(f"Missing columns: {missing}")
    print(f"Loaded {len(df)} rows. Classes:\n{df[TARGET].value_counts().to_string()}")
    return df

def run_pca(df: pd.DataFrame) -> tuple[pd.DataFrame, PCA, np.ndarray]:
    """Standardize features, run PCA, and return a plotting DataFrame and the fitted PCA object."""
    X = df[FEATURES]
    y = df[TARGET]

    # Drop NaNs
    mask = ~(X.isnull().any(axis=1) | y.isnull())
    X, y = X[mask], y[mask]

    X_scaled = StandardScaler().fit_transform(X)

    pca = PCA(n_components=2)
    X_pca = pca.fit_transform(X_scaled)

    ev = pca.explained_variance_ratio_
    print(f"PC1: {ev[0]:.2%}, PC2: {ev[1]:.2%}, Total: {ev.sum():.2%}")

    pca_df = pd.DataFrame(X_pca, columns=['PC1', 'PC2'])
    pca_df[TARGET] = y.values
    return pca_df, pca, X_scaled

def plot_scores(pca_df: pd.DataFrame, explained_variance: np.ndarray):
    """Scatter plot of PCA scores, colored by class."""
    unique_classes = sorted(pca_df[TARGET].unique())
    colors = Category10[max(3, len(unique_classes))][: len(unique_classes)]
    color_map = dict(zip(unique_classes, colors))

    p = figure(
        width=800, height=600,
        title="PCA Scores — SDSS Objects (ugriz colors)",
        tools=[HoverTool(tooltips=[("Class", "@class"), ("PC1", "@PC1{0.00}"), ("PC2", "@PC2{0.00}")]),
               "pan", "wheel_zoom", "box_zoom", "reset", "save"],
    )
    for cls in unique_classes:
        p.circle('PC1', 'PC2',
                 source=ColumnDataSource(pca_df[pca_df[TARGET] == cls]),
                 size=8, alpha=0.7, color=color_map[cls], legend_label=str(cls))

    p.xaxis.axis_label = f'PC1 ({explained_variance[0]:.1%} variance)'
    p.yaxis.axis_label = f'PC2 ({explained_variance[1]:.1%} variance)'
    p.legend.title = 'Object Class'
    p.legend.click_policy = "hide"
    show(p)


def plot_loadings(pca: PCA, explained_variance: np.ndarray):
    """Biplot-style loadings plot showing feature contributions."""
    loadings = pca.components_.T  # (n_features, 2)

    p = figure(
        width=600, height=600,
        title="PCA Loadings — Feature Contributions",
        tools="pan,wheel_zoom,box_zoom,reset,save",
    )

    for i, feat in enumerate(FEATURES):
        x, y = loadings[i, 0], loadings[i, 1]
        p.line([0, x], [0, y], line_width=2, color='red', alpha=0.8)
        p.triangle([x], [y], size=10, color='red', alpha=0.8)
        p.text([x * 1.15], [y * 1.15], text=[feat], text_font_size="12pt",
               text_align="center", text_baseline="middle")

    # Reference circle and axes
    theta = np.linspace(0, 2 * np.pi, 100)
    p.line(np.cos(theta), np.sin(theta), line_color='gray', line_alpha=0.4, line_dash='dashed')
    p.line([-1, 1], [0, 0], line_color='gray', line_alpha=0.3)
    p.line([0, 0], [-1, 1], line_color='gray', line_alpha=0.3)

    lim = np.abs(loadings).max() * 1.3
    p.x_range.start, p.x_range.end = -lim, lim
    p.y_range.start, p.y_range.end = -lim, lim
    p.xaxis.axis_label = f'PC1 ({explained_variance[0]:.1%})'
    p.yaxis.axis_label = f'PC2 ({explained_variance[1]:.1%})'
    show(p)


def plot_scree(X_scaled: np.ndarray):
    """Scree plot showing individual and cumulative explained variance per PC."""
    pca_full = PCA().fit(X_scaled)
    ev = pca_full.explained_variance_ratio_ * 100
    cumev = np.cumsum(ev)
    n = len(ev)
    pcs = list(range(1, n + 1))

    p = figure(
        width=600, height=400,
        title="Scree Plot — Astronomical Dataset",
        tools="pan,wheel_zoom,box_zoom,reset,save",
        x_range=(0.5, n + 0.5),
    )

    # Individual variance bars
    p.vbar(x=pcs, top=ev, width=0.5, alpha=0.7,
           color='steelblue', legend_label='Individual explained variance')

    # Cumulative variance step line
    p.line(pcs, cumev, line_width=2, color='red',
           legend_label='Cumulative explained variance')
    p.circle(pcs, cumev, size=7, color='red')

    p.xaxis.axis_label = 'Principal Component'
    p.yaxis.axis_label = 'Explained Variance (%)'
    p.xaxis.ticker = pcs
    p.legend.location = 'center_right'
    p.y_range.start = 0
    p.y_range.end = 110  # headroom for the cumulative line

    show(p)

# --- MAIN ---
df = load_data(DATA_URL)
pca_df, pca, X_scaled = run_pca(df)
plot_scores(pca_df, pca.explained_variance_ratio_)
plot_loadings(pca, pca.explained_variance_ratio_)
plot_scree(X_scaled)

Take the original Python script and find the line where the data is scaled. Comment it and perform again the PCA analysis. Is there any consequence? Replot and analyze. How much variance is capture now by PC1 and PC2? Do you still see the quasars separation in the scores plot?

Investigate how adding a non-color, physical feature like ‘redshift’ changes the PCA results and the interpretability of the components. Redshift is a measure of how much an object’s light has been stretched as it travels through the expanding universe; it is strongly correlated with distance..  In the original script, add ‘redshift’ to the list of features to be included in the analysis. Re-run the script.
What is the new total explained variance captured by PC1 and PC2? Has it increased or decreased?
Look at the new Scores Plot. Is the separation between the three classes (STAR, GALAXY, QSO) better or worse than in the original plot? Pay close attention to the Star/Galaxy overlap.
Examine the new Loadings Plot. Where does the new ‘redshift’ arrow point? What does its direction and length tell you about its contribution to PC1 and PC2?

Based on the new loadings, what do PC1 and PC2 now represent? Has the interpretation of “overall brightness” or “color contrast” changed? What physical reason might explain why adding redshift was so effective (or ineffective) at separating the classes?
Plot the scores for the first 3 and 4 principal components.
Analyze how the classes (STAR, GALAXY, QSO) are distributed in the 3D space
Calculate the cumulative explained variance for the first 3 or 4 components. How much of the total variance is explained by these components?
Does adding more components improves the separation between classes and whether the additional components add significant information?
Compare the performance of PCA with other dimensionality reduction methods like t-SNE or UMAP.
Apply t-SNE or UMAP to the original feature set (without PCA) and visualize the results.
Compare the ability of t-SNE/UMAP to separate the classes with PCA.
Discuss the advantages and limitations of PCA versus non-linear dimensionality reduction methods like t-SNE and UMAP.

The Iris dataset is a classic in machine learning and statistics. It contains 150 samples from three species of Iris flowers (Setosa, Versicolor, and Virginica). For each sample, four features were measured: sepal length, sepal width, petal length, and petal width.
Your goal is to use PCA to ‘summarize’ these four measurements and visualize the separation between the species. To do so, you can start by importing the needed libs

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris

# Set some default plotting styles for better looking visuals
sns.set_style('whitegrid')

# Load the data
iris = load_iris()
X = iris.data # The feature matrix
y = iris.target # The labels (species)
feature_names = iris.feature_names
target_names = iris.target_names

# Let's look at the first 5 rows of the data
print("Feature Names:", feature_names)
print("\nFirst 5 rows of data:\n", X[:5])
perform the PCA using sklearn and visulize and analyze the results (what components are more important, meaning of the linear compbinations, etc). Include an scores and a biplot plot.
perform PCA “manually” by following each algorithm step. Do you find something different when comparing  with the sklearn approach?
to new coordinates that maximize variance
- use it to understand data structure
Want to visualize or reduce dimensionality
Small sample size relative to variables
